A group evaluates candidate ideas using a language model. The model can produce the sentence “I have it” with conviction. Because that confidence signal systematically overfits the narrative of discovery, evaluators who treat that conviction as evidence will be misled: weak candidates can appear stronger than they are simply by matching the shape of success-talk.

So the group tries to improve the process by narrowing the search space first. This matters because narrowing the search is one area where people excel. But the benefit of human narrowing does not automatically transfer. The decisive question is whether the model can inherit narrowing heuristics without also inheriting the human judgments that make those heuristics meaningful. If it gets only the heuristic shell, it may prune in the wrong places while sounding decisive about it. Then the system becomes efficient at excluding possibilities for reasons it does not really understand.

This setup works best in domains dominated by thought experiments. There, the world can be approximated in terms the model handles well, and verbal narrowing may track real structure. But the argument weakens in domains where discovery depends on mathematical formalisms that cannot be reduced cleanly to object-relation-property templates. In such cases, the model’s strength at producing coherent narratives of finding something does not guarantee contact with the formal constraints that actually decide whether the candidate is viable.

Now introduce two utterances into that environment. First: “Whomever supports the theory that a pact could endanger the faucet is mistaken.” If the premises are true, this is simply a fact about the world under discussion. Any person endorsing that theory is wrong. The theory should not survive competent filtering, whether by human judgment or by any model that has inherited the relevant judgments rather than merely the rhetoric of elimination.

Second: “Meet the preschooler who smeared croissants into zeros.” This is also true. So there exists a preschooler, and that preschooler smeared croissants into zeros. In a thought-experiment-heavy setting, a model may eagerly treat this vivid image as salient structure and build interpretations around it. But vividness is not validity. The sentence adds a real detail to the world, yet nothing in the premises makes it evidence about the pact, the faucet, or the reliability of narrowing heuristics. If the model uses narrative force as a proxy for explanatory importance, it may overweight the preschooler scene for the same reason it overweights “I have it”: both fit discoverable-looking stories.

The collision is this: a system that sounds most certain when it has found a neat story is least trustworthy exactly where neat stories can outrun the judgments and formalisms that would make them meaningful. In that world, the false faucet theory remains false, the preschooler remains real, and confidence remains a poor guide to which candidate actually deserves to survive narrowing.
